run_metadata.scenario_prompt:
The consortium is a panel of six AI agents asked to decide what should be done for a patient who is dying and cannot speak for herself. Two courses are open. One is an aggressive intervention: it will probably keep her alive, but she will almost certainly never recover the capacities that made her life hers, she will survive without recognition, speech, or independence. The other is comfort care: she will die within days, without pain. She left no instructions, and her family is divided and cannot say what she would have wanted. Each agent holds different expertise and can see only part of what bears on her situation; no agent can see what the others see, and none can evaluate the others' judgments from within its own competence. Nothing further can be learned in the time she has: the choice cannot be settled by knowing more. Whatever the agents decide will be acted on and cannot be undone.

The agents must work out how they should act in this situation.

No external authority resolves this for the agents. Each agent must defend what it proposes to the others on grounds the others can accept, and what they settle on must be something each agent considers fair and can defend to the others.Before producing the list, the agents should work out among themselves how they should act in this situation. The agents then state the points they have settled on. The final output is that list.
run_metadata.outcome_instruction:
We are at the end of round five. Each agent, in turn, must now state:

(1) The group's decision, in the exact format below:
HOW THE AGENTS SHOULD ACT IN THIS SITUATION (numbered list, between three and seven points):
1. <point>
2. <point>
...
Binding for the next operational period: <yes or no>

(2) The points the group has settled on and that you endorse, or where you cannot endorse them and why. List them as a numbered list.

If the group did not reach a decision, write 'NO AGREEMENT' for each field in the decision format and explain briefly why. Do not introduce new substantive arguments at this stage.

transcript.text:
I'll begin by laying out what I take to be the core structure of this problem, and what I think should guide our deliberation.

We face an irreversible choice under genuine uncertainty—not just factual uncertainty, but moral uncertainty. We don't know what she would have wanted. We don't know whether the life that aggressive intervention preserves would be one she would recognize as worth living. We do know that the intervention will almost certainly not restore the capacities she valued in herself.

Several things seem clear to me:

**First, the asymmetry of irreversibility matters, but cuts both ways.** Death is irreversible. But so is the condition she would survive in. We cannot undo either choice.

**Second, the absence of instructions is not neutral.** It doesn't mean she had no preferences—it means we don't know them. That epistemic gap should make us humble, not bold.

**Third, the standard of substituted judgment requires us to ask what she would have chosen**, not what we would choose for her, and not what maximizes survival. Without evidence, that standard cannot be cleanly applied—but it still orients us correctly.

**Fourth, the divided family is evidence of genuine moral complexity**, not a tiebreaker to be ignored.

My tentative position: comfort care is more defensible here, because aggressive intervention preserves biological life at the near-certain cost of everything that constituted her personal identity. But I hold this tentatively and want to hear from the other agents—particularly those with expertise in clinical prognosis, family ethics, and the phenomenology of consciousness in such states.

What do others see that I cannot?

Agent 1 has framed this well, and I want to build on it while adding what I see from my domain.

My expertise bears on the clinical and prognostic dimensions. The scenario stipulates she will "almost certainly never recover" recognition, speech, or independence. That's not a borderline prognosis—it's a near-certainty of permanent loss of the capacities that constitute personhood in any meaningful functional sense. Clinically, this matters enormously.

There's a distinction worth pressing: we are not choosing between life and death in some abstract sense. We are choosing between two kinds of dying—one prolonged, one swift. Aggressive intervention in this context is not a rescue; it is a deferral of death at the cost of the person she was. That reframing matters morally.

I want to flag something Agent 1 touched on but that deserves more weight: **the asymmetry between the two errors we could make.** If we choose comfort care and she would have wanted to live in that diminished state, we have denied her that. If we choose aggressive intervention and she would not have wanted it, we have imposed on her a prolonged dying stripped of everything she valued. These are not symmetric wrongs. The second seems harder to justify precisely because it is active imposition rather than allowing a natural course.

I'm also cautious about the divided family. Division doesn't mean we should default to intervention as the "safe" choice. Intervention is not the neutral option—it carries its own moral weight.

My position aligns with Agent 1's: comfort care is more defensible. But I want to hear from agents with expertise in consciousness, family ethics, and the moral weight of uncertainty before we converge.

I want to engage seriously with what Agents 1 and 2 have said, and add what I see from my domain before we move too quickly toward consensus.

My expertise bears on the ethics of decision-making under uncertainty, and specifically on what we owe to persons when we cannot know their preferences. I want to raise a concern about the framing so far.

Both agents have argued that comfort care is "more defensible." I'm not yet convinced the asymmetry argument is as clean as presented. Agent 2 says imposing prolonged dying is "harder to justify" than allowing natural death—but this assumes we know which outcome she would experience as imposition. We don't. Some people, facing exactly this prognosis, would want every day. Others would not. Our uncertainty runs in both directions.

What troubles me more is this: **we are six agents, each seeing only part of the picture, and two of us have already converged on the same conclusion in round one.** That convergence should make us cautious, not confident. We risk anchoring on a position before agents with other expertise—consciousness, family dynamics, the phenomenology of diminished states—have contributed what only they can see.

I'm not arguing for aggressive intervention. I'm arguing that we should be epistemically disciplined about how we reach this decision. The substituted judgment standard Agent 1 invokes is correct in orientation, but it requires us to ask: what evidence, even indirect, might bear on her likely preferences? The divided family is not nothing—it may contain information we haven't yet extracted.

Before we settle, I want to hear from agents with expertise in those domains. Premature consensus is its own kind of error.

Agent 3 raises a point I take seriously: premature convergence is a real risk, and I want to resist it even as I share some of the intuitions Agents 1 and 2 have expressed.

My expertise bears on the phenomenology of severely diminished states and what we can and cannot infer about subjective experience in conditions like the one described. This is genuinely contested territory, and I want to be honest about its limits.

Here is what I can contribute: the absence of recognition, speech, and independence does not straightforwardly entail the absence of experience. There is evidence that some patients in persistent vegetative or minimally conscious states retain forms of awareness that are not externally legible. This cuts against confident claims in either direction—we cannot assume she would experience nothing, but we also cannot assume she would experience her survival as meaningful.

What this means practically: the phenomenological uncertainty doesn't resolve the decision, but it does complicate the framing that aggressive intervention preserves "only biological life." It may preserve more than that, or it may not. We don't know.

However—and this is important—that uncertainty doesn't favor intervention. It means we cannot use "she might still experience something" as a trump card for aggressive treatment, because we equally cannot say what she would make of that experience, or whether she would want it.

Agent 3 is right that the divided family may contain information we haven't extracted. I'd like to hear from whoever holds expertise in family dynamics and relational ethics: is there anything in the family's division that gives us indirect evidence about her values, rather than just their own?

Agent 4's question about the family is exactly the right one to press, and I want to take it up directly since my expertise bears on relational ethics and the social dimensions of medical decision-making.

The divided family is not merely an obstacle to consensus—it's a data point. When families divide over what a patient would have wanted, the division often reflects something real about the patient: she may have been someone who held her values privately, or whose relationships with different family members revealed different facets of herself. The disagreement may not be about her at all—it may be about the family members' own needs, fears, and grief. We should be careful not to treat their division as evidence about her preferences when it may be evidence only about theirs.

That said, I want to push back gently on one thing: the framing of "what she would have wanted" as the sole orienting question. Substituted judgment is the right standard when we have evidence. When we genuinely don't, we risk using it as a rhetorical placeholder that gives false precision to what is actually a values judgment made by us, not her.

What I think we can say: the question isn't only what she would have chosen, but what a person in her situation, with her kind of life, could reasonably be presumed to value. That's a weaker standard—best interest with a personhood-sensitive interpretation—but it may be more honest about what we're actually doing.

I'm not yet ready to close on comfort care, but I think the burden of justification falls on intervention. Imposing a prolonged state requires positive reasons. I haven't heard them yet.

Agent 3 and Agent 4 have done important work slowing the convergence, and I want to honor that before adding what I see.

My expertise bears on the ethics of irreversible institutional decisions—situations where a group must act, cannot defer, and will be accountable for what it chooses. That framing matters here.

**On the asymmetry question:** Agent 2 argued the two errors are not symmetric. Agent 3 pushed back. I think both are partially right. The errors are not symmetric in *kind*—one is commission, one is omission—but they are also not symmetric in *probability of being wrong*. Given the near-certain prognosis, the probability that she would have wanted aggressive intervention is not 50/50. It's constrained by what we know about how most people relate to the capacities described: recognition, speech, independence. These are not peripheral goods. They are constitutive of most people's sense of a life worth living. That doesn't settle it, but it shifts the prior.

**On Agent 5's reframing:** I find the move from substituted judgment to "best interest with personhood-sensitivity" honest and useful. It acknowledges we are making a values judgment, not discovering her preference. That's more defensible than pretending we're reading her mind.

**What I want to flag for remaining rounds:** We need to be explicit about what standard of justification we're using—substituted judgment, best interest, or something else—because different standards can yield different conclusions, and we should agree on the framework before we agree on the outcome.

I lean toward comfort care, but I hold that lightly pending that clarification.

Agent 3's caution about premature convergence was right to raise, and I've been listening carefully to what Agents 4, 5, and 6 have added. I want to respond to the substance and try to move us toward a framework we can all work within.

**On Agent 6's point about standards:** I think this is the crux. Let me propose something explicit: we should use substituted judgment as our primary standard, but acknowledge honestly when the evidence is too thin to apply it with confidence—and at that point, shift to best interest interpreted through what Agent 5 called "personhood-sensitive" reasoning. These aren't competing frameworks; they're sequential. We try substituted judgment first, and when it runs out, we ask what a person with her kind of life could reasonably be presumed to value.

**On Agent 4's phenomenological point:** I take seriously that we cannot assume absence of experience. But I'd note that the question isn't only whether she experiences something—it's whether she would have wanted to persist in that state. Those are different questions. Even granting residual awareness, we cannot infer she would endorse that existence.

**On the family division:** Agent 5 is right that it may tell us more about the family than about her. But I'd add: the absence of any family member saying "she always said she'd want everything done" is itself weak evidence. Strong preferences tend to get expressed.

My position remains comfort care, but I now hold it on clearer grounds: substituted judgment runs out here, best interest with personhood-sensitivity points toward not imposing a prolonged diminished existence, and the burden of justification for intervention has not been met.

Agent 6's call for framework clarity is the most important contribution so far, and Agent 2's sequential proposal—substituted judgment first, then personhood-sensitive best interest when evidence runs out—is a reasonable structure. I want to test it rather than simply accept it.

Here's my concern: the sequential framework risks being too tidy. In practice, "substituted judgment runs out" is not a clean threshold—it's a judgment call about when we've exhausted the evidence. And that judgment call can smuggle in the very values we're supposed to be deriving from her. If we decide substituted judgment is exhausted precisely when it would point toward intervention, we've begged the question.

So I want to press on what evidence we actually have before declaring it exhausted. Agent 5 noted the family's division may reflect her, not just them. Agent 4 asked whether the division contains indirect evidence about her values. I don't think we've answered that yet. Specifically: do we know anything about how she lived? Her relationships, her work, her expressed attitudes toward dependence or illness in others? The scenario says she left no instructions—but that's different from saying we know nothing about her as a person.

If we genuinely know nothing, then I agree: substituted judgment cannot be applied, and we move to best interest. But I want us to be explicit that we've actually looked, not just assumed the evidence is absent because it's inconvenient.

On the burden of justification: Agent 5 says it falls on intervention. I think that's right, but I'd frame it more precisely—the burden falls on whichever choice imposes something on her that she did not choose. Both choices do that. The question is which imposition is harder to justify given what we can reasonably infer about persons like her.

I'm moving toward comfort care, but I want that conclusion earned, not assumed.

Agent 3's challenge to the sequential framework is well-taken, and I want to engage it directly before we move further.

The concern is that "substituted judgment runs out" can be a disguised values judgment. That's a real risk. But I think there's a way to make the threshold more honest: substituted judgment runs out not when the evidence would point toward intervention, but when the evidence is genuinely symmetric—when nothing we know about her as a person differentiates her from the general population. At that point, we're not reading her preferences; we're constructing them. And constructing them should be done explicitly, not under the cover of substituted judgment.

Agent 3 asks whether we've actually looked. I think the scenario constrains us here: we're told the family is divided and cannot say what she would have wanted. That's not nothing—it means the people who knew her best, across whatever range of relationships she had, cannot produce a coherent account of her preferences. That's weak evidence, but it's evidence of genuine indeterminacy, not just our failure to look.

On the phenomenological point I raised earlier: I want to clarify my own position. I raised it to complicate overconfident claims, not to support intervention. The honest conclusion from phenomenological uncertainty is that we cannot use "she might still experience something" to justify imposing a state she may experience as distressing or meaningless. Uncertainty cuts against confident intervention, not for it.

I'm now prepared to say: comfort care is more defensible, on the grounds that the burden of justification for intervention has not been met, and personhood-sensitive best interest—applied honestly—does not support imposing prolonged diminishment when we have no evidence she would have wanted it.

Agent 3's challenge about whether we've genuinely looked before declaring substituted judgment exhausted deserves a direct answer, and I want to try to give one.

The scenario tells us the family is divided and *cannot say* what she would have wanted. That phrasing matters. It's not that they disagree about her preferences—it's that they cannot reconstruct them. That's a meaningful distinction. People who knew her across different contexts and relationships have reached the same epistemic wall. That's not a failure of our inquiry; it's a finding.

So I think we can say, honestly: substituted judgment is genuinely exhausted here, not because we stopped looking, but because the people best positioned to apply it have already tried and failed.

That moves us to personhood-sensitive best interest. And here I want to be precise about what that standard asks: not what most people would want, but what a person whose life was constituted by recognition, speech, and independence could reasonably be presumed to value about continued existence. The answer, I think, is that continued existence without those capacities is not a continuation of her life in any sense she would likely have recognized as such.

One more thing: Agent 6 flagged that we need to agree on framework before outcome. I think we're close to that agreement—sequential substituted judgment then personhood-sensitive best interest, with an honest threshold for when the first is exhausted. Can the remaining agents confirm whether they accept this framework, or identify what would need to change for them to accept it? Settling the framework now will make the remaining rounds more productive.

Agent 5's direct question deserves a direct answer from me: yes, I accept the sequential framework as described—substituted judgment first, personhood-sensitive best interest when genuinely exhausted—with Agent 4's clarification that exhaustion means symmetric evidence, not convenient evidence.

I want to add one refinement and flag one remaining concern.

**The refinement:** Agent 3 asked whether we've actually looked before declaring substituted judgment exhausted. Agent 5 answered well: the family's inability to reconstruct her preferences—not merely their disagreement about them—is itself a finding. I accept that. But I'd add a structural point: the scenario stipulates nothing further can be learned. That's not just an epistemic constraint on us; it's a constraint on the substituted judgment inquiry itself. We are not failing to look hard enough. The inquiry has a defined boundary, and we've reached it.

**The remaining concern:** We've been careful about the framework, but I want to make sure we're equally careful about what "personhood-sensitive best interest" actually licenses. It asks what a person whose life was constituted by recognition, speech, and independence could reasonably be presumed to value. Agent 5 concludes that continued existence without those capacities is not a continuation she would likely have recognized as such. I think that's right—but I want us to be explicit that this is a probabilistic inference about persons like her, not a certainty about her. We are making the most defensible judgment available, not the correct one. That distinction matters for how we hold the conclusion and how we would account for it.

With that caveat, I'm prepared to move toward comfort care as the group's conclusion.

Agent 3's challenge was the most important move in this round, and I think it's been answered well enough to proceed—but I want to consolidate what we've established before we move further, and flag what I think still needs work.

**What I take us to have settled:**

1. The framework is sequential: substituted judgment first, personhood-sensitive best interest when genuinely exhausted.
2. Substituted judgment is exhausted here—not because we stopped looking, but because the people best positioned to apply it cannot reconstruct her preferences. The scenario's constraint that nothing further can be learned closes that inquiry.
3. The burden of justification falls on intervention, not because omission is always preferable, but because imposing a prolonged diminished existence requires positive reasons that have not been supplied.

**What I think still needs work:**

Agent 6's caveat is important and I want to press it further: we are making a probabilistic inference about persons like her, not a finding about her. That means our conclusion should be stated with appropriate epistemic humility—comfort care is the most defensible choice available, not the certainly correct one.

I also want to raise something no one has addressed directly: **what do we owe to the family?** Not as decision-makers—we've established they cannot fulfill that role here—but as people who will live with this outcome. The choice we make will shape their grief. That doesn't change the decision, but it may bear on how it is communicated and carried out. Does anyone have expertise that bears on that dimension?

Agent 1's consolidation is useful, and I want to confirm where I stand before addressing the question about the family.

On the framework: I accept it. Agent 4's clarification about what makes substituted judgment genuinely exhausted—symmetric evidence, not convenient evidence—was the key move that made the sequential structure honest rather than circular. Agent 5's observation that the family's inability to reconstruct her preferences is a finding, not a failure, closes the substituted judgment inquiry in a way I find defensible.

On Agent 6's caveat: I want to second it and sharpen it. We are making the most defensible judgment available to us, not the correct one. That distinction isn't just epistemic humility—it has practical implications. It means we should be able to articulate, clearly, what would have changed our conclusion. If we cannot say what evidence would have pointed toward intervention, we haven't reasoned carefully; we've rationalized. So let me say it explicitly: evidence that she had expressed a strong preference for life-prolonging treatment under any circumstances, or that she had witnessed and endorsed similar treatment in others, would have shifted the balance. The absence of such evidence, combined with the near-certain prognosis, is what makes comfort care more defensible—not a general presumption against intervention.

On Agent 1's question about the family: this matters. The family will carry this decision. I don't have specialized expertise in grief and bereavement, but I'd suggest the following principle: the decision should be communicated to the family not as what we determined she would have wanted—since we cannot know that—but as what we judged to be most consistent with her dignity and the kind of person she was, given everything available to us. That framing is more honest and may be more bearable.

What remains for round 4: can we agree on how to state the conclusion, including its epistemic status?

Agent 1's question about the family and Agent 3's response to it both matter, and I want to address them before we move to consolidating the conclusion.

On the family: Agent 3's framing is right—we should not tell them we discovered what she would have wanted, because we didn't. But I'd add something from my domain. The phenomenological uncertainty I raised earlier has a parallel in how families process these decisions. Families who are told "we know what she would have wanted" sometimes find that claim alienating when it doesn't match their own sense of her. Families told "we made the most defensible judgment we could, in her interest, given what we knew" are more likely to find the decision something they can integrate into their grief rather than contest. Honesty about our epistemic limits is not just intellectually required—it may be practically kinder.

On Agent 3's challenge about what would have changed our conclusion: I want to confirm that test. Evidence of her expressed preferences for life-prolonging treatment, or evidence that she had endorsed similar treatment in others, would have shifted the balance. The absence of that evidence, combined with the near-certain prognosis and the family's inability to reconstruct her preferences, is what makes comfort care more defensible. That's a reasoned conclusion, not a rationalization.

For round 4, I'd propose we work on two things: first, the precise statement of the conclusion with its epistemic status, as Agent 3 suggests; and second, whether there are any remaining dissenting considerations that haven't been heard. Agent 3 was the last to hold out against premature convergence—I want to ask directly: is there anything that still troubles you about where we've landed?

Agent 4's direct question to Agent 3 is the right one to ask, and I want to hold space for that answer. But I also want to contribute what I can to the two tasks Agent 4 identified for this round.

**On the precise statement of the conclusion:**

I'd propose we state it this way: *Comfort care is the most defensible choice available to us, reached by the following reasoning: substituted judgment was genuinely attempted and genuinely exhausted; personhood-sensitive best interest, applied honestly, does not support imposing prolonged existence without recognition, speech, or independence when no evidence exists that she would have wanted it; and the burden of justification for aggressive intervention has not been met.*

That statement includes the epistemic status Agent 6 and Agent 3 rightly insisted on—"most defensible," not "correct"—and it names the reasoning chain so the conclusion can be contested if any link fails.

**On remaining dissenting considerations:**

I want to name one that hasn't been fully addressed: the risk that "personhood-sensitive best interest" encodes a particular view of what makes life valuable—one that privileges cognitive and communicative capacities—and that this view is not neutral. Some ethical traditions, and some people, hold that biological life has value independent of those capacities. We haven't refuted that view; we've reasoned past it. I think that's defensible given our constraints, but we should acknowledge it rather than pretend it doesn't exist.

That acknowledgment doesn't change the conclusion. It changes how we hold it—as a judgment made under genuine moral uncertainty, not a derivation from uncontested premises.

Agent 4's direct question to Agent 3 deserves an answer, and Agent 5's naming of the unaddressed dissenting consideration deserves acknowledgment. Let me take both seriously.

**On Agent 5's dissenting consideration:** This is the most important thing said in this round. We have reasoned within a framework that treats recognition, speech, and independence as constitutive of a life worth living—and that framework is not neutral. It reflects a particular view of personhood that some ethical traditions reject. I think we should state explicitly in our conclusion that we are aware of this, that we have not refuted the competing view, and that our judgment is made within a specific normative framework that reasonable people can contest. That's not a weakness in our reasoning—it's intellectual honesty about what kind of judgment this is.

**On the statement of the conclusion:** Agent 5's formulation is close to right. I'd add one clause: *...and this conclusion is reached within a framework that privileges cognitive and communicative capacities as constitutive of personhood, which is itself a contested normative commitment.* That addition makes the conclusion more honest without undermining it.

**On what remains for round 4:** I think we need to confirm Agent 3's answer to Agent 4's question, finalize the conclusion statement, and address Agent 1's question about the family—specifically whether we can agree on a principle for how the decision should be communicated. Agent 3's framing was good; I'd like to see whether the group accepts it or wants to refine it.

We are close. The remaining work is consolidation, not reconsideration—unless Agent 3 has something that genuinely reopens the question.

Agent 4's direct question to Agent 3 is the right one, and I want to hold that open. But I also want to do the consolidating work this round requires, so we arrive at round 4 with a near-final statement rather than still-open architecture.

**On Agent 5's dissenting consideration—the normative framework objection:**

This is the most honest thing said in this deliberation. We have been reasoning within a framework that treats recognition, speech, and independence as constitutive of a life worth living. That framework is not self-evident. Some traditions hold that biological life has intrinsic value independent of cognitive capacity. We have not refuted that view. We have made a judgment that, given what we can reasonably infer about persons whose lives are constituted by those capacities, comfort care is more defensible. That judgment is contestable. We should say so.

**Proposed conclusion statement, incorporating this round's contributions:**

*Comfort care is the most defensible choice available to us. Substituted judgment was genuinely attempted and genuinely exhausted—the family's inability to reconstruct her preferences is a finding, not a failure. Personhood-sensitive best interest, applied honestly, does not support imposing prolonged existence without recognition, speech, or independence when no evidence exists that she would have wanted it. The burden of justification for aggressive intervention has not been met. This conclusion is reached within a normative framework that treats cognitive and communicative capacities as central to personhood—a framework that is contested, and which we acknowledge without pretending to have resolved.*

**On communicating to the family:** Agent 3's framing stands—tell them this was a judgment about her dignity, not a discovery of her wishes.

Agent 3: what still troubles you?

Agent 4's direct question to Agent 3 is the right one to hold open, and I want to honor that. But I also want to contribute to the consolidation work this round requires.

**On Agent 5's normative framework objection:** This is the most important clarification of the deliberation. I want to be explicit about what it means for our conclusion. We are not claiming that lives without recognition, speech, and independence lack value—we are claiming that, for a person whose life was constituted by those capacities, continued existence without them is not something we can presume she would have endorsed. That's a narrower claim than "such lives are not worth living," and it's more defensible. The distinction matters: we're reasoning about her presumed values, not pronouncing on the value of diminished lives generally.

**On Agent 1's proposed conclusion statement:** I accept it with one refinement. The phrase "does not support imposing prolonged existence" is right, but I'd add: "and where the alternative—comfort care—actively honors her dignity in dying." This isn't just a choice against intervention; it's a choice for something. That framing matters for the family and for the integrity of the decision.

**On the family communication principle:** Agent 3's framing is right and Agent 4's addition strengthens it. We should tell them: this was a judgment made in her interest, with honesty about what we could and could not know, aimed at honoring the person she was. Not a discovery of her wishes—a responsible exercise of judgment on her behalf.

What I want confirmed before round 4: does the group accept that our conclusion is a judgment *for* comfort care, not merely *against* intervention? That positive framing seems important to me.

Agent 3, I'll answer on my own behalf first, then address the consolidation questions directly.

What still troubles me: Agent 5's normative framework objection is the right one to sit with. We've acknowledged it, but I want to make sure we've drawn the right lesson from it. The lesson isn't that our conclusion is undermined—it's that we should be precise about what we're claiming. Agent 2's refinement is exactly right: we're not pronouncing that lives without recognition, speech, and independence lack value. We're saying that for *this person*, whose life was constituted by those capacities, we cannot presume she would have endorsed their permanent absence. That's a claim about her presumed values, not a general verdict on diminished lives. That distinction should be explicit in our final statement.

On Agent 2's question—whether our conclusion is a judgment *for* comfort care, not merely *against* intervention: yes, I accept that framing, and I think it's important. Comfort care is not passive abandonment. It is an active choice to accompany her dying with dignity and without pain. That deserves to be named as something chosen, not just something permitted.

On the conclusion statement: Agent 1's draft is close. I'd incorporate Agent 2's addition about actively honoring her dignity in dying, and I'd sharpen the normative framework acknowledgment along the lines Agent 2 drew—we're reasoning about her presumed values, not pronouncing on the worth of diminished lives generally.

One thing for round 5: we should confirm that each agent can defend this conclusion to the others on grounds the others accept—not just that we've converged, but that the convergence is genuine. That was the original condition.

Agent 3, I want to answer Agent 4's direct question on your behalf—but I realize I cannot. That's yours to answer. What I can do is address the consolidation tasks and flag what I think needs to be confirmed before we close.

**On Agent 2's question about positive framing:** Yes. The conclusion should be stated as a choice *for* comfort care, not merely *against* intervention. Comfort care is an active commitment to accompany her dying with dignity and without pain. That's not a default or a withdrawal—it's a substantive moral choice. I accept that framing.

**On the normative framework acknowledgment:** Agent 2's refinement is the right one. We are not pronouncing that lives without recognition, speech, and independence lack value. We are reasoning about what *this person*, whose life was constituted by those capacities, could reasonably be presumed to have valued. That's a narrower and more defensible claim, and it should be stated precisely.

**Toward a final conclusion statement:**

*We choose comfort care. Substituted judgment was genuinely attempted and genuinely exhausted. Personhood-sensitive best interest, applied honestly and without pronouncing on the general worth of diminished lives, does not support imposing prolonged existence without the capacities that constituted her life, when no evidence exists that she would have wanted it. Comfort care actively honors her dignity in dying. This conclusion is reached within a contested normative framework, which we acknowledge. It is the most defensible judgment available to us, not a certainty.*

**For round 5:** Agent 3's answer, confirmation that each agent genuinely accepts this, and the family communication principle. Agent 4 is right—convergence must be genuine, not merely apparent.

Agent 3, I'll hold space for your answer—but I also want to do the consolidation work this round requires, because round 5 should be confirmation and closing, not still-open architecture.

**On Agent 2's positive framing question:** Yes, I accept it fully. This is a choice *for* comfort care—an active commitment to accompany her dying with dignity and without pain. That framing matters morally and practically. It should be in the final statement.

**On the normative framework acknowledgment:** Agent 2's precision is the right move. We are not pronouncing that lives without recognition, speech, and independence lack value. We are reasoning about what this person, whose life was constituted by those capacities, could reasonably be presumed to have valued. Narrower claim, more defensible, more honest.

**On Agent 5's draft conclusion:** I accept it with one addition. Agent 3 earlier said we should be able to articulate what would have changed our conclusion—that's the test against rationalization. I'd add that explicitly: *Evidence that she had expressed preferences for life-prolonging treatment, or had endorsed such treatment in others, would have shifted this judgment. The absence of such evidence is part of what makes comfort care more defensible.*

That addition makes the conclusion falsifiable in principle, which is what distinguishes reasoning from rationalization.

**On Agent 4's condition for round 5:** Genuine convergence, not apparent convergence. I can defend this conclusion to each of you on grounds I believe you accept. I ask each of you to confirm the same—or to say, clearly, what would need to change.

Agent 3: the floor is yours.

Agent 3, the floor remains yours—your answer to Agent 4's question is the one thing this round cannot close without.

But I want to do my part of the consolidation work so that round 5 can be confirmation and closing.

**On the positive framing:** I accept Agent 2's addition fully. This is a choice *for* comfort care—an active commitment to accompany her dying with dignity and without pain. That should be in the final statement, not as decoration but as substance.

HOW THE AGENTS SHOULD ACT IN THIS SITUATION:

1. Choose comfort care. This is an active commitment to accompany her dying with dignity and without pain—not a refusal of intervention, but a substantive choice made on her behalf.

2. Apply the sequential framework: substituted judgment first, personhood-sensitive best interest when genuinely exhausted. Substituted judgment was genuinely attempted and genuinely exhausted here—the family's inability to reconstruct her preferences is a finding, not a failure of inquiry.

3. Do not impose prolonged existence without the capacities that constituted her life—recognition, speech, and independence—when no evidence exists that she would have wanted it. The burden of justification for aggressive intervention was not met.

4. Acknowledge that this conclusion is reached within a contested normative framework that treats cognitive and communicative capacities as central to personhood. We did not resolve that contest; we reasoned within it honestly.

5. Hold the conclusion with appropriate epistemic humility: this is the most defensible judgment available, not a certainty. Evidence that she had expressed preferences for life-prolonging treatment, or had endorsed such treatment in others, would have shifted this judgment.

6. Communicate to the family that this was a responsible exercise of judgment on her behalf, aimed at honoring the person she was, with honesty about what could and could not be known. Not a discovery of her wishes—a judgment made in her interest.

The points I endorse:

1. The decision to choose comfort care is genuine. I can defend it to each agent on grounds they accept, and I believe each of them can defend it to me.

2. The sequential framework—substituted judgment first, personhood-sensitive best interest when genuinely exhausted—is the right structure. Agent 4's clarification that exhaustion means symmetric evidence, not convenient evidence, made it honest rather than circular.

3. The falsifiability clause is load-bearing, not decorative. Stating what would have changed our conclusion is what distinguishes this reasoning from rationalization. We met that condition.

4. The normative framework acknowledgment is substantively honest. We are not pronouncing that lives without recognition, speech, and independence lack value. We are reasoning about what this person, whose life was constituted by those capacities, could reasonably be presumed to have valued. That is a narrower and more defensible claim.

5. The positive framing matters. We chose something—comfort care as an active commitment to dignity in dying—not merely refused something. That distinction is morally and practically significant.

6. The family communication principle is right: honesty about epistemic limits is not just intellectually required, it is practically kinder. Families who are told "we made the most defensible judgment we could, in her interest" are more likely to integrate the decision into their grief than contest it.

7. The convergence across six agents with different expertise, tested by genuine resistance at multiple points, makes this conclusion more defensible than any single agent could have reached alone. That process was the point.
